terms.definition: The degree to which the parts of a social system maintain mutually reinforcing alignment across time
The constant annual percentage growth rate that would yield the observed total change over a multi-year period
The length of time required for a population growing exponentially to double in size, approximated by ln(2)/r where r is the annual growth rate
The average number of live births a woman would have over her lifetime given current age-specific birth rates
The proportion of individuals born into a group who remain members as adults
A culturally sanctioned period of relative freedom in late adolescence during which Amish youth may experience life outside the community before choosing baptism
The sociological proposition that groups imposing higher costs of membership achieve higher commitment and cohesion
The unwritten but binding code of conduct governing all aspects of Amish life
A Christian movement originating in 16th-century Europe that advocates adult baptism and a separation from state authority
Collective term for Amish, Old Order Mennonite, and related groups distinguished by plain dress and separation from mainstream culture
An Amish entrepreneur whose net worth equals or exceeds one million U.S. dollars, typically through manufacturing or real estate rather than farming
Colloquial label for wage-earning Amish who work hourly factory jobs instead of owning land or businesses
A theological ethic of yieldedness and humility central to Amish social norms
